meta.title: Jury Reports Decoder
meta.scope: Economic and commercial CPGE programmes, and the scientific CPGE programmes covered by the examination banks listed below.
meta.principle: Official links are detected automatically. Teaching summaries are reviewed before publication.

do: Answer the exact question and follow the required format.
Prioritise information instead of reproducing the document in its original order.
Build a genuinely personal, reasoned and well-illustrated commentary.
Use precise, accurate and relevant examples.
Aim for clear, correct and natural English before trying to sound sophisticated.
Leave enough time for genuine interaction and listen carefully to the questions.
Proofread or self-correct: basic grammar, spelling, literal translations and false friends.
avoid: Paraphrasing, narrating or describing without analysing.
Forcing a pre-learned lesson, introduction or plan onto the document.
Reciting idiomatic expressions that you do not fully control.
Confusing a summary, a synthesis and a personal commentary.
Inventing quotations, figures or references.
Using an automatic “advantages versus disadvantages” plan.
Speaking at length to conceal a lack of substance.
Reading notes instead of communicating with the examiners.
recommend: Practise under the exact time conditions of the examination you are preparing.
Read and listen to English-language news regularly, then check facts through reliable sources and the school library.
Turn each comment from a teacher or examiner into one precise learning target.
Practise spontaneous speech, pronunciation and interaction, not only the prepared presentation.
Compare several reports from the same examination to identify recurring comments.
Actively reuse in class and in colles the vocabulary and knowledge prepared on this site.

warnings.label: Pre-learned material forced onto the task

competitions.name: Banque Commune d’Épreuves — ELVi
format: ELVi written paper: comparative analytical summary, argumentative essay and translation, depending on the language and level.
There is no single oral format: arrangements depend on the individual schools within the examination bank.
Word limits and the distinction between the different tasks are assessed strictly.
do: Compare the documents genuinely in the analytical summary, without adding a personal opinion.
Answer the essay question clearly and adopt a nuanced position.
Use the source material without merely repeating it, then add accurate references.
Translate meaning, register and nuance rather than isolated words.
Respect the word limit and organise the answer into readable paragraphs.
avoid: Turning the comparative summary into an essay.
Adding personal commentary to the first task.
Forcing irrelevant course material onto the topic.
Accumulating poorly controlled idiomatic expressions.
Inventing or misattributing a quotation.
recommend: Practise each subtask separately.
Time the division between summary, essay, translation and proofreading.
Build a bank of verified examples organised by major themes in the English-speaking world.
Systematically review core grammar structures.

competitions.name: ECRICOME PRÉPA
format: Written paper: annotated past papers explaining the purpose of the task, marking criteria, model answers and examiner feedback.
Oral examination: video document, preparation, structured account, commentary and interview.
The examination rewards both linguistic control and clear, well-reasoned expression.
do: Contextualise the document precisely from the introduction.
Keep the account of the document clearly separate from the commentary.
Build the commentary around a relevant key question.
Leave enough time for genuine discussion with the examiners.
Adapt your knowledge to the document and the exact topic.
avoid: Narrating the video image by image.
Mechanically announcing a memorised plan or reciting a spectacular hook.
Repeating in the commentary what has already been said in the account.
Giving minimal answers during the interview.
Using overambitious but inaccurate language.
recommend: Practise with short current-affairs videos.
Develop efficient visual and audio note-taking.
Prepare natural transitions between the account, commentary and interview.
Record yourself, then correct pace, pronunciation and speaking time.

competitions.name: Concours CentraleSupélec
format: Written paper: a synthesis in the foreign language based on a multi-document dossier.
Oral examination: choose between two recent articles, prepare for 20 minutes, speak for no more than 10 minutes, then take part in an interview.
Examiners assess the key question, prioritisation, synthesis, range and linguistic accuracy.
do: Formulate a key question that covers the whole dossier.
Cross-reference the documents and prioritise the ideas.
Act as a neutral mediator in the written synthesis, without giving a personal opinion.
At the oral, produce a concise account followed by a reasoned, well-illustrated commentary.
Keep approximately ten minutes for interaction.
avoid: Placing separate mini-summaries of each document side by side.
Using should or must to impose an opinion in a neutral synthesis.
Adding artificial headings and subheadings.
Neglecting sources or visual documents.
Reading a text written during preparation.
recommend: Practise reformulation and language showing convergence and divergence.
Train yourself to produce an informative title and a complete key question.
Read recent articles for oral practice and develop knowledge of the English-speaking world.
Use an authorised non-connected watch to calibrate the presentation.

competitions.name: Concours commun Mines-Ponts
format: Written and oral examiner reports are published separately.
An official methodology supplement explains the expectations of the English written paper.
Candidates should consult the annual reports as well as the past papers.
do: Follow the exact nature of the task and every instruction.
Build a clear line of reasoning supported by relevant evidence.
Show independence and use the examiner’s prompts constructively.
Pay attention to linguistic quality and clarity of communication.
Prepare the full two-year syllabus and current affairs in the English-speaking world.
avoid: Restricting preparation to previous years’ papers.
Neglecting the methodology specific to this examination.
Rejecting or ignoring avenues suggested by the examiner.
Accumulating ideas without linking them.
Confusing quantity of speech with quality of content.
recommend: Read the methodology supplement before beginning the past papers.
Compare the written and oral examiner reports.
Turn each recurring comment into a targeted exercise.
Practise explaining and defending an idea rather than reciting.

competitions.name: Concours commun INP
format: A common modern-language written paper is accompanied by session reports.
Oral examination: listen to a document, give a structured account, present a commentary and take part in an interview.
The 2025 report describes a 25-minute examination and expects 15 to 20 minutes of continuous speaking.
do: Present the source, date and subject of the document in the introduction.
Give a structured, non-chronological account.
Clearly signal the move to the commentary and announce its organisation.
Step back from the document, broaden the discussion with examples and defend a viewpoint.
Manage the time so that the interview has a genuine place.
avoid: Automatically stopping after 8 to 10 minutes as in some classroom colles.
Delivering listening notes in an unstructured list.
Skipping the personal commentary.
Forcing a commentary that is unrelated to the document.
Speaking at length without substance.
recommend: Practise presentations lasting 15 to 20 minutes.
Prepare flexible transitions and commentary structures.
Work on eye contact, pace, posture and spontaneity.
Review basic grammar errors and question formation.

competitions.name: Banque e3a-Polytech
format: Language papers are published in the examination bank’s past-paper archive.
Some written papers are shared with other examination banks; oral examinations are organised by individual schools or groups of schools.
Candidates must therefore check the current regulations and the arrangements of each oral examination centre.
do: Check which examinations are actually used by the schools you are targeting.
Read the current regulations as well as the past papers.
Practise the linguistic accuracy and synthesis required in the written paper.
Prepare separately for each school’s oral format.
Check every year whether any papers are shared with CCINP.
avoid: Assuming that all schools use the same oral format.
Confusing a paper, model answer and examiner report.
Preparing a generic oral format without checking the current regulations.
Relying on an old format that has not been confirmed.
Neglecting school-specific instructions.
recommend: Use this page as a gateway to current official sources.
Create a personal fact sheet for each target school.
Check arrangements as soon as the new regulations are published.
Ask your teacher to help prioritise the examinations you are actually preparing.

competitions.name: Banque Filière PT
format: Compulsory English oral in 2025: 20 minutes of preparation followed by a 20-minute presentation and interview.
The presentation should not exceed 12 minutes, leaving meaningful time for interaction.
The archive separates written reports, oral reports and past papers.
do: Summarise the main points and the links between them.
Build a problem-driven, nuanced and well-illustrated commentary.
Communicate with the examiners, vary your tone and show genuine interest.
Be able to explain every word or expression you use.
Leave sufficient time for the interview.
avoid: Reading from over-detailed notes.
Reciting a learned speech instead of discussing.
Using a theatrical, artificial hook.
Building a binary advantages-versus-disadvantages commentary.
Using stereotypes or unsupported generalisations.
recommend: Practise spontaneous expression on abstract topics.
Work on the pronunciation and stress of frequent words.
Deliberately limit the presentation to ten or twelve minutes.
Practise genuine interviews with follow-up questions and deeper discussion.

reports.label: Banque PT English LV1 oral examiner report 2025

competitions.name: École polytechnique / ENS
format: Oral examination: a 4-to-6-minute current-affairs video, 30 minutes of preparation and a 20-minute examination.
Give a precise, structured account, then a problem-driven commentary and an interview.
Examiners assess comprehension, linguistic precision, independent language use and quality of thought.
do: Use the sound, images, tone and construction of the sequence.
Find the right balance between main ideas and precise details.
Build the commentary around a genuine key question.
Conclude promptly when the examiner asks you to do so.
Leave approximately ten minutes for interaction.
avoid: Exceeding ten minutes for the initial presentation.
Describing the video without structuring the account.
Introducing new examples in a delayed conclusion.
Ignoring the visual elements or media context.
Reciting a commentary without adapting to questions.
recommend: Practise with the official videos published online.
Use an authorised timer or watch.
Practise accurate reformulation of figures, roles and proper names.
Develop independent, extended answers during the interview.

reports.label: Papers and examiner reports — École Polytechnique
English oral examiner report — MP / MPI / PC / PSI

actions.warning: The examiners identify paraphrase or a linear account of the document.
actions.work: Methodology → identify, prioritise and reformulate the essential ideas.

actions.warning: The commentary lacks examples or contextual knowledge.
actions.work: Civilisation → build accurate examples connected to a clear issue.

actions.warning: The language sounds artificial or imprecise.
actions.work: Vocabulary → Study, Memorise and Challenge, then reuse the language in class and in colles.

actions.warning: The presentation lacks structure or spontaneity.
actions.work: Mission Builder → practise under examination conditions, self-evaluate precisely and send weak points to Review Studio.
Resources and the school library → verify facts, cross-check sources and deepen your knowledge.
